title: Europe has promised to end its reliance on Russian gas. Yet it is sending billions to Moscow for Arctic LNG
content:
In the first four months of the year, European countries imported a record 91 tankers from the Yamal terminal and paid Moscow an estimated 3.88 billion euros for the gas.

Following the loss of most of its pipeline gas customers in Europe, Russia has turned to liquefied natural gas (LNG) as its main weapon in the energy market. The Russian Yamal terminal produces this fuel directly in the Arctic and ships it to European ports via tankers.

In the first four months of this year, virtually all of its exports were destined for Europe, with only two ships sailing to China. European countries received a record 91 tankers and paid Moscow an estimated €3.88 billion for the gas.

According to preliminary data for the first half of 2026, this figure is already approaching 6 billion euros. This business continues despite the European REPowerEU plan to completely cut ties with Russian energy.

However, long-standing ties are preventing the termination of these supplies: the French giant TotalEnergies still owns a one-fifth stake in the Siberian plant, and this investment is tied to long-term purchase contracts that European companies are reluctant to terminate prematurely.
snippet: In the first four months of the year, European countries imported a record 91 tankers from the Yamal terminal and paid Moscow an estimated 3.88 billion euros for the gas.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Romanian authorities mull allowing pension funds to invest in the defence sector
content:
The legislative proposal introduces the possibility for private pension fund assets (Pillar II – Romania’s mandatory private pension system) to be invested in companies operating in the defence sector and in dual-use technologies, while maintaining the ban on financing companies involved in the production or sale of weapons prohibited under international treaties.

The initiative is based on the premise of “the current strategic and economic reality, in which national security and economic resilience are deeply interconnected”, according to the bill’s explanatory memorandum.

Romania’s National Defence Strategy for 2025–2030 explicitly recognises the role of the national economy in the security architecture, emphasising that the economy must generate resources for military procurement programmes and directly contribute to the production of military equipment and technology, including through the development of dual-use capabilities, according to the initiators.
snippet: The legislative proposal introduces the possibility for private pension fund assets (Pillar II – Romania’s mandatory private pension system) to be invested in companies operating in the defence sector
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Czech Coalition Split Over Funding for U.S. Weapons for Ukraine
content:
Prime Minister Andrej Babiš (ANO) sought to play down the significance of the contribution, describing it as “a symbolic, one-off payment that will not be repeated.” Nevertheless, the Czech Republic’s decision to join the Prioritised Ukraine Requirements List (PURL) initiative has quickly become the center of a political row.

Just ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara, Foreign Minister Petr Macinka (Motorists) announced that the Czech Republic would contribute about €5.8 million to the fund through which allied nations purchase U.S.-made weapons for Ukraine. The announcement caught not only the public but also many politicians off guard. While President Petr Pavel welcomed the decision, Macinka’s coalition partners from SPD reacted angrily.

Tomio Okamura, leader of SPD and Speaker of the Chamber of Deputies, publicly distanced himself from the initiative.

“SPD firmly opposes the Czech Republic’s participation in an initiative under which European countries finance U.S.-made weapons for Ukraine. This issue was never discussed by the coalition council. It was a unilateral decision by the foreign minister,” Okamura wrote on the social media platform X.
snippet: Prime Minister Andrej Babiš (ANO) sought to play down the significance of the contribution, describing it as “a symbolic, one-off payment that will not be repeated.” Nevertheless, the Czech Republic’s
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Travel rebound: Central Europe’s winners and losers
content:
Europe welcomed 5% more international tourists in the first months of 2026 compared with the same period last year, while the number of overnight stays increased by 4.8%, according to the latest quarterly report, European Tourism: Trends & Prospects, published by the European Travel Commission (ETC).

The report notes that demand for travel to Europe remains resilient despite weakening consumer confidence, rising living costs and disruptions to air travel caused by the conflict in the Middle East.

Nearly 80% of the destinations covered by the report recorded an increase in visitor arrivals, while around one in five achieved double-digit growth. The strongest gains were reported by traditional holiday destinations, with Greece (+38%), Italy (+21%) and Malta (+16%) leading the way. According to the ETC, improved air connectivity and efforts to spread tourism beyond the peak summer season and the most popular destinations played a key role in driving this growth.

Northern Europe posted the strongest regional performance, with international arrivals rising by 10% and overnight stays by 8.4%. Finland recorded a 10.4% increase in arrivals, followed by Norway (7.1%) and Sweden (6.3%).
snippet: Europe welcomed 5% more international tourists in the first months of 2026 compared with the same period last year, while the number of overnight stays increased by 4.8%, according to the latest
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: The European Food Safety Authority’s Bovaer report is delayed
content:
A new comprehensive European investigation into Bovaer, the additive designed to reduce methane emissions from ruminants, has been delayed. The additive has sparked widespread debate and opposition in the Nordic countries due to its alleged adverse effects.

The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) had originally planned to publish a new scientific assessment of Bovaer by June 30. It had already assessed Bovaer in 2021, when the additive was found to be safe for cows and was not considered a cause for concern for consumers when used in accordance with recommendations. The new scientific assessment will focus particularly on practical experience with the use of Bovaer.

The assessment is significantly delayed. EFSA informed Faktabaari on June 30 that it had requested an extension from the European Commission for the assessment. According to EFSA’s new timeline, the assessment will be completed by the end of this year.
snippet: A new comprehensive European investigation into Bovaer, the additive designed to reduce methane emissions from ruminants, has been delayed. The additive has sparked widespread debate and opposition in
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How Russia exploits institutional silence to fracture public trust in Romania
content:
Focusing on the drone strike in Galați on 29 May 2026 and the naval drone explosion in the Port of Constanța on 5 June 2026, the analysis shows how Moscow exploited moments of uncertainty to deepen public distrust, amplify polarization, and weaken confidence in Romanian institutions.

The Galați incident (29 May) marked a turning point. A Russian Geran-2 drone struck a residential building, injuring civilians and exposing both operational and communication vulnerabilities. While the physical damage was limited, the delay in official explanations created an information vacuum that was quickly filled with conspiracy theories, false-flag accusations, and coordinated online manipulation.

Just days later, the Constanța incident followed a similar pattern. Although a Ukrainian naval drone had been diverted by Russian electronic warfare before self-detonating in the port, pro-Kremlin narratives reframed the event as evidence of Ukrainian aggression or Romanian government complicity.
snippet: Focusing on the drone strike in Galați on 29 May 2026 and the naval drone explosion in the Port of Constanța on 5 June 2026, the analysis shows how Moscow exploited moments of uncertainty to deepen
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Diplomacy by parcel: When orders and gifts become political weapons
content:
In recent weeks, relations between Poland and Ukraine have taken an unusual turn, sparked by a dispute over one of Poland’s highest state honours, the Order of the White Eagle. 

The controversy unfolded after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky approved the naming of a Ukrainian military unit after the “Heroes of the Ukrainian Insurgent Army (UPA)”, a World War II-era nationalist formation that remains deeply controversial in Poland due to its role in wartime events in Volhynia and Eastern Galicia. 

Polish historians and state institutions hold UPA units responsible for the mass killing of Polish civilians during the Volhynia massacres of 1943–1945, an episode widely referred to in Poland as the “Volhynia genocide”, while in Ukraine the same events are often interpreted in a more complex and contested historical context, including the broader war conditions and local violence of the period.
snippet: In recent weeks, relations between Poland and Ukraine have taken an unusual turn, sparked by a dispute over one of Poland’s highest state honours, the Order of the White Eagle.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: China’s quiet push into Poland: What is Beijing looking for?
content:
Behind the scenes of Chinese diplomacy, attitudes towards Poland are often tinged with condescension. In Chinese public discourse, Poland is sometimes mockingly described as a “small big country”—a medium-sized state that, despite its limited weight, aspires to play the role of a major power on the international stage.

From the perspective of a global power like China, Poland might appear to be little more than a peripheral player. Yet geopolitical realities force Beijing to think otherwise. Like elsewhere across the European Union, China pursues strategic interests in Poland. The key lies in the EU’s decision-making architecture, where Beijing has long sought to exploit divisions among member states through a classic divide-and-rule strategy.

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: The Cohesion Policy Paradox: Closing the Gap Between Countries, Not Within Them
content:
Negotiations over the EU’s Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF) have traditionally pitted net contributors against net beneficiaries. At the heart of the dispute lies a fundamental disagreement over the purpose of the EU budget.

The largest spending programmes have long been agricultural subsidies and cohesion policy. The willingness of so called frugal member states to continue financing these programmes depends largely on whether they deliver tangible results and contribute to broader European priorities.

The primary objective of EU cohesion policy is to reduce economic, social and territorial disparities between regions across the Union – a goal enshrined in the EU treaties themselves. As a result, the main beneficiaries are so-called cohesion countries, whose gross national income (GNI) per capita remains below 90% of the EU average.

Most of these countries joined the EU in 2004, and accession marked the beginning of a period of rapid economic growth. Poland’s economic convergence with Western Europe is among the EU’s most notable success stories. Its GDP per capita rose from roughly 51% of the EU average in 2004 to around 80–81% in 2024–2025.
snippet: Negotiations over the EU’s Multiannual Financial Framework (MFF) have traditionally pitted net contributors against net beneficiaries. At the heart of the dispute lies a fundamental disagreement over
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Will the EU be flooded with genetically modified food under the new rules?
content:
At the same time, a number of organisations have sharply criticised the changes, arguing that they will reduce food safety standards and deprive consumers of the right to choose the food they wish to grow, buy and eat because products containing new genetically modified organisms (GMOs) will no longer be identified on labels. Delfi explains whether the EU has, in fact, granted food producers unrestricted rights to cultivate and market new GMOs without oversight.

On the 17th of June, the European Parliament (EP) approved rules which, according to the Parliament itself, will make it easier to develop plants that are resistant to adverse weather conditions and pests, produce higher yields and require fewer pesticides.

The rules regulate new gene-editing techniques for plants, known as new genomic techniques (NGTs). Plants developed using these techniques will be divided into two categories depending on how and to what extent their genomes have been modified. Plants with minor genetic changes that could also have arisen through conventional breeding will be treated as conventional plants, while all other plants will remain subject to the same strict rules that currently apply to GMOs.
